heroOverview: Polyphagia (ICD-10: R63.2) is a state of abnormally increased appetite and hunger that drives eating beyond normal caloric needs, often without a corresponding sense of satiety. Together with polydipsia and polyuria, it forms the classic triad of uncontrolled diabetes mellitus; however, polyphagia also occurs in hyperthyroidism, Prader-Willi syndrome, Cushing's syndrome, certain psychiatric conditions, and as a side effect of medications such as corticosteroids and antipsychotics. The physiological mechanism involves either insufficient cellular glucose uptake — as in insulin-deficient or insulin-resistant diabetes, where cells are starved despite high blood glucose — or dysregulation of appetite-signalling hormones including ghrelin, leptin, and GLP-1. Identifying the underlying cause determines whether treatment is hormonal, dietary, pharmacological, or psychiatric.

keyStats.prevalence: Polyphagia is present in approximately 60% of newly diagnosed type 1 and type 2 diabetes patients; hyperthyroidism causes polyphagia in 40–60% of affected individuals (American Thyroid Association, 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Occurs in all age groups; diabetic polyphagia peaks at ages of highest diabetes incidence; corticosteroid-induced polyphagia affects patients of any age on high-dose or long-term steroid therapy
keyStats.avgAge: Depends on underlying cause; no single peak age
keyStats.globalCases: Hundreds of millions worldwide affected annually as a symptom of diabetes and hyperthyroidism

definition: Polyphagia (ICD-10: R63.2) is the clinical symptom of excessive hunger or increased appetite leading to food intake well above normal caloric requirements. It is regulated by the hypothalamic hunger centres, which integrate peripheral signals from ghrelin (hunger-stimulating), leptin (satiety-signalling), insulin, GLP-1, PYY, and CCK. Pathological polyphagia results when cells are glucose-deprived despite adequate or excess food intake — as in uncontrolled diabetes mellitus, where insulin deficiency prevents glucose entry into cells — or when appetite-suppressing signals fail. In hyperthyroidism, an accelerated basal metabolic rate creates a caloric deficit that drives hunger. In Cushing's syndrome, excess cortisol drives fat redistribution and persistent appetite stimulation. In Prader-Willi syndrome, a hypothalamic defect eliminates normal satiety. Medication-induced polyphagia occurs with corticosteroids, atypical antipsychotics (particularly olanzapine and clozapine), cyproheptadine, and some antihistamines. Unlike binge eating disorder, polyphagia as a symptom does not necessarily involve loss of control or distress around eating, though the conditions can coexist. Management requires diagnosis of the underlying cause, targeted treatment, and, where needed, structured dietary and behavioural intervention.

typesClassification.description: The most common cause; cellular glucose starvation due to insulin deficiency (type 1) or insulin resistance (type 2) drives persistent hunger even after eating; accompanies polydipsia and polyuria.
Excess thyroid hormone raises basal metabolic rate by 20–80% above normal; the resulting energy deficit creates genuine hunger; weight loss typically accompanies increased food intake.
Corticosteroids, atypical antipsychotics (olanzapine, clozapine), cyproheptadine, and some antihistamines stimulate appetite through central mechanisms; often causes significant weight gain.

Damage to or congenital abnormalities of the hypothalamic satiety centres — from tumours, surgery, trauma, or genetic disorders such as Prader-Willi syndrome — eliminates the normal off-switch for hunger.
Anxiety, mood disorders, and stress can dysregulate the HPA axis and drive comfort or emotional eating; this overlaps with binge eating disorder but is distinct from it as a clinical category.

primarySymptoms: Persistent hunger that returns rapidly after a full meal, distinguishing pathological polyphagia from normal appetite.
Eating quantities substantially larger than usual without reaching satiety, often consuming two to three times normal portion sizes.
Frequent eating, with an inability to wait more than one to two hours between meals or snacks.
Weight change — loss in diabetic and hyperthyroid polyphagia (caloric loss exceeds gain), gain in medication-induced and hypothalamic polyphagia.
Fatigue and irritability when food is not available, reflecting the metabolic instability driving the hunger signal.
Polydipsia and polyuria accompanying polyphagia in uncontrolled diabetes mellitus, forming the classic diagnostic triad.
Heat intolerance, palpitations, and tremor in hyperthyroid polyphagia, distinguishing it from diabetic polyphagia.
Difficulty concentrating and light-headedness between meals, reflecting the energy imbalance or hypoglycaemia driving hunger.
Preoccupation with food, including persistent thoughts about eating even shortly after a large meal.
In Prader-Willi syndrome: food-seeking behaviour, food hoarding, and inability to perceive fullness.
earlyWarningSigns: Eating noticeably more than usual over several consecutive days without an increase in physical activity.
Feeling hungry again within one hour of a full meal, on multiple occasions.
Waking at night with hunger strong enough to prompt eating.
Rapid unintentional weight loss despite an apparent increase in food intake — a strong signal for new-onset diabetes or hyperthyroidism.
emergencySigns: Weight loss greater than 10% over one to two months alongside polyphagia and polydipsia — suggests uncontrolled type 1 diabetes requiring same-day evaluation.
Signs of diabetic ketoacidosis: fruity breath, deep rapid breathing, vomiting, and confusion alongside polyphagia.
Thyroid storm in hyperthyroid polyphagia: very high fever, rapid heart rate above 140 beats per minute, altered consciousness, and vomiting.
Hypoglycaemia driving polyphagia: pallor, sweating, rapid heartbeat, and confusion, which requires immediate glucose replacement.

causes.description: In insulin deficiency (type 1) or insulin resistance (type 2), glucose cannot enter muscle and fat cells efficiently; despite elevated blood glucose, the cells signal starvation to the hypothalamus, which responds with persistent hunger. The resulting polyphagia worsens hyperglycaemia further without effective treatment.

Excess thyroid hormone (T3, T4) accelerates the basal metabolic rate and cellular energy consumption; the widening gap between energy intake and expenditure drives hunger signals in the hypothalamus, producing polyphagia that typically coexists with weight loss, heat intolerance, and palpitations.

Glucocorticoids stimulate appetite directly through hypothalamic neuropeptide Y activation and through insulin resistance that reduces cellular glucose uptake; high-dose or prolonged corticosteroid use causes significant and often distressing weight gain through increased appetite and preferential visceral fat deposition.

Olanzapine, clozapine, quetiapine, and risperidone block hypothalamic histamine H1 and serotonin 5-HT2C receptors, both of which normally suppress appetite; the resulting hunger is difficult to voluntarily resist and contributes substantially to metabolic syndrome in this patient population.

A genetic disorder caused by loss of paternally expressed genes on chromosome 15q11-q13; absent or insufficient satiety signalling from the hypothalamus produces insatiable hunger beginning in early childhood, leading to severe obesity unless food access is strictly controlled.

Blood glucose below approximately 3.5–4.0 mmol/L activates counter-regulatory hormones and a powerful hunger drive; reactive hypoglycaemia after high-carbohydrate meals, or iatrogenic hypoglycaemia in insulin-treated diabetes, creates recurrent polyphagia that can be confused with a primary appetite disorder.

Chronic stress elevates cortisol and activates ghrelin, displacing leptin's satiety effect and driving preference for calorie-dense foods; stress-related polyphagia is among the most common contributors to weight gain in the general population and may progress to binge eating disorder.

riskFactors.description: Poor glycaemic control perpetuates cellular glucose starvation; restoring normal glucose levels with insulin or oral agents resolves polyphagia in days to weeks.
Patients on olanzapine or clozapine have particularly high rates of weight gain and polyphagia; switching to lower-metabolic-risk agents (aripiprazole, ziprasidone) can mitigate this.
Appetite stimulation is dose-dependent; using the lowest effective steroid dose, alternate-day dosing, or steroid-sparing agents reduces polyphagia.
Antithyroid therapy (carbimazole, propylthiouracil) or radioiodine ablation restores normal metabolism and eliminates hunger excess within weeks to months.
Polyphagia is a defining feature due to a fixed hypothalamic defect; environmental restriction of food access is the primary management strategy.

Damage to the ventromedial hypothalamus from craniopharyngioma, surgery, or radiation therapy permanently disrupts satiety signalling, producing hyperphagia that is resistant to most pharmacological treatments.
Autonomous insulin secretion from a pancreatic beta-cell tumour causes recurrent hypoglycaemia, which drives polyphagia; surgical removal of the insulinoma is curative.
Effective treatment of underlying mood and anxiety disorders with psychotherapy and, where appropriate, pharmacotherapy reduces stress-driven polyphagia.

affectedDemographics: Adults with newly diagnosed or poorly controlled diabetes mellitus (types 1 and 2)
Children and adolescents with type 1 diabetes at diagnosis
Patients with hyperthyroidism, particularly Graves' disease
Individuals taking long-term corticosteroids or atypical antipsychotics
People with Prader-Willi syndrome (genetic, present from childhood)
Adults with chronic stress, anxiety, or depression who use food as a coping mechanism
diagnosisOverview: Polyphagia is a symptom that directs investigation toward underlying metabolic, endocrine, or psychiatric causes. The clinical history captures the duration, pattern, associated symptoms (weight change direction, polydipsia, polyuria, heat intolerance, palpitations, mood changes), and all current medications. The physical examination notes BMI trend, signs of hyperthyroidism (tremor, goitre, tachycardia, warm skin), Cushing's syndrome (moon face, central obesity, striae, proximal myopathy), and neurological signs of hypothalamic disease. Initial blood tests include fasting glucose and HbA1c to detect diabetes mellitus; thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) and free T4 to detect hyperthyroidism; morning cortisol or 24-hour urinary free cortisol to screen for Cushing's syndrome; and a full blood count. If insulinoma is suspected — polyphagia with documented hypoglycaemia, often with a low BMI — a supervised 72-hour fast with simultaneous glucose, insulin, C-peptide, and proinsulin measurements is the gold-standard test. For suspected hypothalamic polyphagia, brain MRI evaluates the hypothalamus and pituitary. Psychiatric assessment with validated tools (PHQ-9, SCOFF screen for eating disorders) completes the workup when metabolic causes are excluded.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses diabetes mellitus, the most common cause of polyphagia; HbA1c provides the chronic glycaemic picture.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw after overnight fasting; results within hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for hyperthyroidism, where excess thyroid hormone increases metabolic rate and drives hunger.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same blood draw as glucose; results within one to two days.

diagnosticTests.test: Morning serum cortisol or 24-hour urinary free cortisol
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for Cushing's syndrome when polyphagia is accompanied by rapid weight gain, stretch marks, and easy bruising.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood taken early morning; urine cortisol requires a 24-hour collection in a provided container.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects autonomous insulin secretion from an insulinoma by documenting low glucose, high insulin, and high C-peptide at symptomatic hypoglycaemia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed in hospital with close monitoring; the fast is terminated when glucose drops below 2.5 mmol/L or symptoms develop.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies structural lesions — craniopharyngioma, pituitary adenoma, hypothalamic tumour — that disrupt satiety signalling.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30–45 minute scan with no radiation; contrast may be given; requires lying still.

diagnosticTests.test: Eating disorder and psychiatric screening (PHQ-9, SCOFF, GAD-7)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies depression, anxiety, and eating disorder contributions to abnormal appetite when organic causes are excluded.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Short questionnaires completed verbally or in writing; no physical test required.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of polyphagia is directed at the underlying cause. In diabetes mellitus, insulin (type 1) or combination glucose-lowering therapy with GLP-1 agonists and metformin (type 2) restores cellular glucose availability, eliminating the starvation signal driving hunger; polyphagia resolves within days to weeks of adequate glycaemic control. In hyperthyroidism, antithyroid medication, radioiodine, or thyroidectomy normalises metabolic rate and appetite within weeks to months. For corticosteroid-induced polyphagia, using the lowest effective dose or steroid-sparing agents reduces appetite stimulation; dietary counselling helps manage weight gain during unavoidable steroid use. When atypical antipsychotics are driving polyphagia, switching to a lower-metabolic-risk agent where clinically safe, and implementing structured dietary and physical activity support, is the standard approach. For hypothalamic obesity and Prader-Willi syndrome, environmental controls on food access are the primary intervention; GLP-1 agonists (exenatide, liraglutide) show promise in small trials. Insulinoma polyphagia resolves after surgical resection. In all cases, a registered dietitian provides structured meal plans that maintain satiety, limit caloric excess, and prevent weight complications.

medicalTreatments.name: Insulin therapy (type 1 diabetes)
medicalTreatments.description: Replaces absent endogenous insulin, restores cellular glucose uptake, eliminates the starvation signal driving polyphagia, and resolves the diabetic triad of polydipsia, polyuria, and polyphagia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Polyphagia resolves in nearly 100% of type 1 diabetes patients when adequate insulin dosing achieves HbA1c below 53 mmol/mol (7%).

medicalTreatments.name: GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide)
medicalTreatments.description: Increase insulin secretion, suppress glucagon, and slow gastric emptying while acting on hypothalamic receptors to significantly reduce hunger and food intake; particularly effective for type 2 diabetes with polyphagia and excess weight.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Semaglutide reduces average daily caloric intake by 20–35% and produces 5–15% body weight loss in randomised trials of type 2 diabetes patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Antithyroid medications (carbimazole, propylthiouracil)
medicalTreatments.description: Block thyroid hormone synthesis; as T3 and T4 normalise over four to eight weeks, metabolic rate returns to normal and polyphagia with associated weight loss resolves.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: TSH normalisation within 4–12 weeks in most patients; polyphagia resolves alongside thyroid function in more than 85% of treated individuals.

medicalTreatments.name: Switching antipsychotic medications
medicalTreatments.description: Replacing olanzapine or clozapine with aripiprazole or ziprasidone where clinically appropriate reduces appetite stimulation substantially; requires psychiatric supervision to ensure psychiatric stability is maintained.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Switching from olanzapine to aripiprazole reduces weight gain by approximately 2–5 kg over 12 weeks in randomised trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Dietary counselling with structured meal plans
medicalTreatments.description: A registered dietitian designs meal plans with high protein and fibre content, balanced macronutrients, and regular eating intervals; structure and satiety-promoting nutrients reduce the drive to overeat even when appetite regulation is pharmacologically impaired.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Structured dietary intervention reduces caloric excess by 15–25% in clinical trials of medication-induced polyphagia and reduces weight gain rates.

surgicalOptions.name: Insulinoma resection
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of the insulin-secreting pancreatic tumour that is causing recurrent hypoglycaemia-driven polyphagia; laparoscopic distal pancreatectomy or enucleation depending on tumour location.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Biochemical cure with resolution of hypoglycaemia and polyphagia in greater than 90% of patients with benign insulinoma after complete resection.

surgicalOptions.name: Thyroidectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of the thyroid gland for hyperthyroidism not controlled by medications or not suitable for radioiodine; resolves excess thyroid hormone production and the resulting polyphagia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Cure of hyperthyroidism in over 98% of patients; polyphagia resolves within weeks as metabolic rate normalises; lifelong thyroid hormone replacement is required post-surgery.

alternativeTreatments.name: High-fibre, high-protein dietary pattern
alternativeTreatments.description: Dietary protein increases satiety hormones (GLP-1, PYY) and reduces ghrelin; fibre slows gastric emptying and stabilises blood glucose; both strategies can partially counteract medication-induced polyphagia without pharmacological change.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mindful eating training
alternativeTreatments.description: Cognitive-behavioural techniques that help patients recognise true hunger versus psychogenic urges, implement structured eating intervals, and avoid conditioned responses to stress and emotion; evidence supports reductions in binge eating episodes of 50–60% in structured programmes.

whySeeSpecialist: Polyphagia accompanied by unintentional weight loss, excessive thirst, or very frequent urination needs same-day evaluation to exclude new-onset diabetes, particularly type 1. Polyphagia alongside palpitations, weight loss, and anxiety warrants thyroid function testing. When polyphagia is causing significant weight gain, distress, or disordered eating behaviours, an endocrinologist, psychiatrist, or specialist dietitian should be involved. Prader-Willi syndrome and hypothalamic obesity are complex conditions requiring a multidisciplinary team.
doctorSelectionGuide: A general practitioner can manage initial evaluation and most straightforward causes of polyphagia. For diabetes-related polyphagia, a diabetologist or diabetes specialist nurse provides optimised insulin and GLP-1 therapy. For hyperthyroid polyphagia, an endocrinologist manages thyroid treatment. For antipsychotic-related polyphagia, a metabolic psychiatrist or psychiatrist familiar with medication switching and lifestyle interventions is best placed to balance psychiatric and metabolic needs.
preventionStrategies: Maintain optimal glycaemic control in diabetes to prevent the cellular starvation signal that drives polyphagia.
Monitor thyroid function annually in at-risk groups (family history, autoimmune disease) to detect hyperthyroidism before polyphagia and weight loss develop.
Use the lowest effective dose of corticosteroids and introduce steroid-sparing agents early when long-term treatment is needed.
For patients starting atypical antipsychotics, establish baseline weight, BMI, and glucose, and provide dietary and physical activity support proactively from the first prescription.
Implement structured environmental food controls early in Prader-Willi syndrome — before the severe hyperphagia phase begins in late infancy.
Treat anxiety and depression promptly, as unresolved psychological distress is a common driver of stress-related polyphagia and weight gain.
lifestyleModifications: Eat protein at every meal (eggs, lean meat, legumes, dairy) to maximise satiety hormone release and reduce meal-to-meal hunger.
Include high-fibre vegetables and whole grains to slow gastric emptying and stabilise blood glucose.
Avoid skipping meals, which worsens hunger spikes and drives compensatory overeating.
Keep a food and hunger diary to identify patterns and distinguish true hunger from conditioned or emotional eating.
Create environmental barriers to overconsumption — smaller plates, portion-controlled containers, keeping high-calorie foods out of sight.
Engage in regular physical activity, which improves insulin sensitivity in diabetes and reduces appetite dysregulation in stress-related polyphagia.

recommended: High-protein foods at each meal (eggs, Greek yoghurt, lean meat, fish, legumes) to promote satiety
Non-starchy vegetables and high-fibre grains to slow digestion and stabilise glucose
Three structured meals and two planned snacks daily to prevent extreme hunger between meals
Low glycaemic index carbohydrates to reduce postprandial glucose spikes that drive reactive hunger
avoid: Highly processed, energy-dense, low-nutrient foods that spike glucose rapidly and resolve hunger only briefly
Skipping meals, which amplifies hunger signals and worsens compensatory overeating
Sugary drinks, which deliver rapid calories without activating satiety hormones effectively
Eating in response to stress, boredom, or emotion rather than true hunger signals

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise (at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity activity per week) improves insulin sensitivity in type 2 diabetes, reducing the cellular glucose deficiency that drives polyphagia. Resistance training increases muscle mass, which improves glucose uptake. In hyperthyroid polyphagia, exercise should be moderate and heart-rate monitored given existing tachycardia; intensity can increase as thyroid function normalises. For antipsychotic-related polyphagia, structured exercise programmes demonstrably reduce weight gain and improve metabolic parameters; supervised classes or gym programmes with accountability improve adherence.
dailyManagement: Patients with diabetes: check blood glucose and take medication consistently; monitor hunger as a signal of poor glycaemic control.
Track food intake and hunger levels daily for the first few weeks after a dietary plan is initiated.
Weigh weekly at the same time to detect weight trends early.
Report rapid weight loss with continued polyphagia to a clinician promptly.
In medication-induced polyphagia: attend regular metabolic monitoring appointments (weight, glucose, lipids) as recommended.
Use structured mealtime environments — sitting at a table without screens, eating slowly — to improve satiety signal recognition.
prognosis: The prognosis of polyphagia depends on the underlying cause. In diabetes mellitus, polyphagia resolves with glycaemic control and carries no independent adverse prognosis beyond that of the underlying diabetes. In hyperthyroidism, polyphagia resolves completely once the thyroid disorder is treated; weight and appetite typically normalise within months. In medication-induced polyphagia, appetite partially improves with dietary measures and dose optimisation, though complete resolution often requires medication change. Hypothalamic polyphagia from craniopharyngioma or Prader-Willi syndrome is a chronic, highly challenging condition associated with severe obesity, metabolic syndrome, and poor quality of life without intensive, sustained management. GLP-1 agonists and structured environment controls improve outcomes in these groups. Insulinoma polyphagia has an excellent prognosis after successful tumour resection.
recoveryTimeline: In diabetes mellitus, polyphagia begins to ease within 24–72 hours of effective insulin or glucose-lowering therapy, and most patients experience hunger returning to near-normal within one to two weeks of achieving glycaemic targets. In hyperthyroidism, hunger typically normalises within four to eight weeks of antithyroid medication producing TSH normalisation. Medication-induced polyphagia may improve partially within weeks of dose reduction or switch. Insulinoma polyphagia resolves within days of successful surgical resection.
complications: Obesity and metabolic syndrome when polyphagia from medications or hypothalamic injury causes sustained caloric excess and weight gain.
Poor glycaemic control in diabetes — polyphagia-driven overeating worsens hyperglycaemia in a self-reinforcing cycle.
Binge eating disorder developing from stress-related or medication-induced polyphagia in predisposed individuals.
Cardiovascular risk from weight gain, dyslipidaemia, and insulin resistance associated with chronic polyphagia from antipsychotics or steroids.
Psychosocial distress, shame, and social withdrawal from inability to control food intake in hypothalamic and medication-induced polyphagia.

supportResources.description: Practical guidance on recognising and managing hunger as a symptom of diabetes for patients and carers.

supportResources.name: PWSA (UK) — Prader-Willi Syndrome Association
supportResources.description: Support and resources for individuals and families living with Prader-Willi syndrome-related hyperphagia.

supportResources.name: American Thyroid Association — Hyperthyroidism
supportResources.description: Patient information on hyperthyroidism symptoms including polyphagia, diagnosis, and treatment options.

confusedWithConditions.name: Binge eating disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Binge eating disorder is characterised by recurrent episodes of eating large amounts with a loss of control and significant distress; polyphagia is a persistent hunger symptom from a medical or pharmacological cause and does not require the episodic loss-of-control pattern.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bulimia nervosa
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bulimia nervosa involves bingeing followed by compensatory behaviours such as vomiting or laxative use, driven by body-image concerns; polyphagia drives increased intake but not the compensatory cycle of bulimia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Polydipsia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Polydipsia is excessive thirst and fluid intake; polyphagia is excessive hunger and food intake. Both occur together in uncontrolled diabetes but are distinct symptoms and can occur independently.

relatedConditions.name: Polydipsia
relatedConditions.relevance: Polydipsia and polyphagia together with polyuria form the classic triad of uncontrolled diabetes mellitus.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 diabetes
relatedConditions.relevance: Type 2 diabetes is the most common cause of polyphagia; cellular insulin resistance creates persistent hunger despite high blood glucose.

relatedConditions.name: Hyperthyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: Excess thyroid hormone accelerates metabolic rate and drives polyphagia, typically with concurrent weight loss.

relatedConditions.name: Binge eating disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Binge eating disorder can coexist with or develop from medication-induced or stress-related polyphagia.

faqs.question: What is polyphagia?
faqs.answer: Polyphagia is abnormally excessive hunger driving food intake well above normal caloric needs, often without reaching satiety. It is one of the three classic symptoms of uncontrolled diabetes alongside excessive thirst and excessive urination. It also occurs in hyperthyroidism, from medications like steroids and antipsychotics, and in hypothalamic disorders.

faqs.question: What causes polyphagia?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is uncontrolled diabetes mellitus, where cells cannot take up glucose and signal starvation to the brain. Other causes include hyperthyroidism, which accelerates metabolism; corticosteroids and atypical antipsychotics, which stimulate appetite directly; hypothalamic disorders; and reactive hypoglycaemia.

faqs.question: Is always being hungry a sign of diabetes?
faqs.answer: Persistent hunger, especially combined with excessive thirst, frequent large-volume urination, and unexpected weight loss, is a classic presentation of uncontrolled diabetes mellitus. A blood glucose test or HbA1c will confirm or exclude this within hours and should not be delayed.

faqs.question: How is polyphagia treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment targets the underlying cause. In diabetes, insulin or GLP-1 agonists restore cellular glucose and eliminate hunger. In hyperthyroidism, antithyroid therapy normalises metabolic rate and appetite. When medications cause it, dose reduction or switching agents reduces the effect. Dietary structure from a registered dietitian helps in all cases.

faqs.question: Can stress cause excessive hunger?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chronic stress elevates cortisol, which increases ghrelin and reduces leptin, driving hunger and preference for high-calorie foods. Stress-related polyphagia often leads to weight gain and can progress to binge eating disorder in predisposed individuals. Treating the underlying stress or anxiety reduces the hunger drive.

faqs.question: What is the difference between polyphagia and binge eating disorder?
faqs.answer: Polyphagia is a persistent, driven hunger from a medical or pharmacological cause. Binge eating disorder is a psychiatric condition involving episodic consumption of large amounts of food with a feeling of loss of control and subsequent distress. The two can coexist but have different causes and treatments.

faqs.question: Does hyperthyroidism cause excessive hunger?
faqs.answer: Yes. Excess thyroid hormone increases basal metabolic rate by 20–80%, creating a genuine caloric deficit that drives persistent hunger. Polyphagia in hyperthyroidism is typically accompanied by weight loss, not gain, which distinguishes it from other causes.

faqs.question: Can medications cause excessive hunger?
faqs.answer: Yes. Corticosteroids, olanzapine, clozapine, cyproheptadine, and some antihistamines are among the most common medication causes. They stimulate appetite centrally and often cause significant weight gain. A pharmacist or clinician review can identify and potentially modify the contributing drug.

faqs.question: What tests are done for polyphagia?
faqs.answer: The primary tests are fasting blood glucose and HbA1c for diabetes, and TSH with free T4 for hyperthyroidism. If steroids or Cushing's syndrome is suspected, a cortisol test is added. Brain MRI is ordered when hypothalamic disease is possible. Psychiatric screening completes the evaluation if medical causes are excluded.

faqs.question: Does polyphagia cause weight gain?
faqs.answer: It depends on the cause. In diabetes and hyperthyroidism, polyphagia coexists with weight loss because the underlying metabolic dysfunction wastes calories. In medication-induced, hypothalamic, and stress-related polyphagia, caloric intake exceeds needs and weight gain often results.

faqs.question: How quickly does polyphagia resolve with diabetes treatment?
faqs.answer: In type 1 diabetes, polyphagia begins to ease within 24–72 hours of effective insulin therapy and fully resolves within one to two weeks of stable glycaemic control. In type 2 diabetes, GLP-1 agonists and other glucose-lowering drugs typically reduce hunger substantially within one to four weeks of treatment initiation.

faqs.question: Is polyphagia the same as hyperphagia?
faqs.answer: The terms are used interchangeably. Hyperphagia is the preferred term in some genetic and neurological contexts (e.g., Prader-Willi syndrome, hypothalamic obesity), while polyphagia is the ICD-coded clinical symptom (R63.2). Both describe abnormally excessive food intake driven by dysregulated hunger.

faqs.question: Can antidepressants cause polyphagia?
faqs.answer: Some antidepressants, particularly mirtazapine and certain tricyclics, increase appetite and cause weight gain as side effects. This is sometimes used therapeutically in patients with anorexia and depression. SSRIs more commonly cause mild appetite suppression initially, though some patients experience increased appetite after the first few weeks.

faqs.question: What is Prader-Willi syndrome and why does it cause polyphagia?
faqs.answer: Prader-Willi syndrome is a genetic disorder from chromosome 15 abnormality that causes a permanent defect in hypothalamic satiety signalling. Affected individuals never feel full regardless of how much they eat. Without strict environmental food controls, they develop severe obesity and its complications from early childhood.

faqs.question: Can polyphagia go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Polyphagia caused by acute stress or a temporary medication may ease once the trigger resolves. However, persistent polyphagia from diabetes, hyperthyroidism, or structural hypothalamic disease will not resolve without treating the underlying cause. Self-resolving polyphagia lasting more than two weeks should still be medically evaluated.

faqs.question: What is the connection between polyphagia and insulin resistance?
faqs.answer: In insulin resistance, muscle and fat cells cannot take up glucose efficiently despite normal or elevated insulin. The brain's energy-sensing neurons detect cellular glucose deficiency and signal hunger. This is why people with insulin-resistant type 2 diabetes often feel hungry soon after a meal containing a large carbohydrate load.

faqs.question: Does polyphagia affect sleep?
faqs.answer: Polyphagia can disrupt sleep through nocturnal hunger, blood glucose fluctuations, and the discomfort of overeating late at night. In diabetes, nocturnal hypoglycaemia can wake the patient with hunger and sweating. Optimising glycaemic control and establishing a consistent evening meal schedule typically improves sleep quality.

faqs.question: When should I seek help for excessive hunger?
faqs.answer: Seek medical evaluation if you have had unexplained excessive hunger for more than two weeks, especially if combined with weight loss, excessive thirst, frequent urination, or palpitations. These combinations suggest an underlying medical cause that needs prompt diagnosis and treatment rather than dietary adjustment alone.

faqs.question: Can GLP-1 agonists treat polyphagia?
faqs.answer: GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide) reduce hunger by slowing gastric emptying and acting on hypothalamic appetite centres. They are highly effective for diabetes-related and obesity-related polyphagia, and are showing promise in medication-induced and hypothalamic polyphagia in clinical trials.

faqs.question: What is the link between polyphagia and polyuria?
faqs.answer: In diabetes mellitus, both are driven by hyperglycaemia. High blood glucose causes osmotic diuresis (polyuria), which triggers thirst (polydipsia). Simultaneously, cellular glucose starvation drives polyphagia. The three symptoms together — polydipsia, polyuria, polyphagia — form the classic triad that should prompt an urgent glucose test.

metaDescription: Polyphagia (R63.2) is persistent excessive hunger caused by diabetes, hyperthyroidism, or medications. Learn symptoms, causes, and treatments. Doctor-reviewed.
